750	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
751	
752	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
753	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
754	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
755	here.
756	
757	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
758	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
759	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact, CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex, CategoryTheory.cosyzygyKernelFork, CategoryTheory.cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles, CategoryTheory.epi_toCycles_of_exactAt}
760	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
762	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
763	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
765	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
766	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
767	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
768	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
769	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
770	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
771	  \[
772	    0 \longrightarrow A
773	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
774	      \longrightarrow \cdots
775	  \]
776	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
777	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
778	  \[
779	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
780	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
781	  \]
782	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
783	  \[
784	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
785	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
786	  \]
787	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
788	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
789	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
790	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
791	\end{lemma}
792	
793	\begin{proof}
794	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
795	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
796	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
797	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
798	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
799	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
800	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
801	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
802	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
803	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
804	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
805	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
806	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
807	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
808	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
809	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
810	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
811	\end{proof}
812	
813	\begin{lemma}[Applied cosyzygy is the cocycle object of the applied complex]
814	  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}
815	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_hom_iCycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_toCycles}
816	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
817	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
818	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D) — the left-exact initial segment
819	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C)$ specialized to the cosyzygy sequences
820	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
821	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
822	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
823	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
824	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
825	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
826	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
827	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
828	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
829	  \[
830	    G(Z^n) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr),
831	  \]
832	  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
833	  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
834	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
839	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
840	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
841	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
842	  \[
843	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
844	  \]
845	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
846	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
847	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
848	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
849	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
850	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
851	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
852	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
853	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
854	  kernels under precomposition, so
855	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
856	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
857	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
858	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
859	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
860	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
861	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
862	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
863	\end{proof}
864	
865	\begin{lemma}[Cohomology of the applied resolution as a cosyzygy cokernel]
866	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
867	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso, CategoryTheory.Functor.gHomologyZeroIso}
868	  % NOTE (iter-007 review): this lemma states BOTH the degree-0 case (H⁰ ≅ G(A)) and the
869	  % positive-degree case (Hⁿ ≅ coker). The Lean split is unavoidable — the n=0 and n≥1 targets
870	  % have different types — so the \lean{} names both decls: cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso (n≥1)
871	  % and gHomologyZeroIso (n=0). Both are sorry-free + axiom-clean.
872	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
873	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
874	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
875	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
876	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
878	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
879	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
880	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
881	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
882	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
883	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
884	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
885	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
886	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
887	  \[
888	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
889	    \qquad
890	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
891	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
892	  \]
893	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
894	  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).
895	\end{lemma}
896	
897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
899	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
907	  \]
908	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
913	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
914	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
915	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
916	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
920	\end{proof}
921	
922	\section{The acyclic-resolution comparison theorem}
923	
924	\begin{theorem}\leanok
925	[An acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor]
926	  \label{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}
927	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution}
928	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution,
929	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker,
930	    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
931	  % NOTE (iter-009 review): the Lean encodes the augmentation in the
932	  % "augmentation-dropped" form `(K, e : A ≅ K.cycles 0, hexact : ∀ n, K.ExactAt (n+1))`,
933	  % equivalent to the blueprint's augmented complex `A → K•`; left-exactness of `G`
934	  % is `[PreservesFiniteLimits G]`. Verified faithful by the iter-009
935	  % lean-vs-blueprint-checker (PASS, 0 red flags).
936	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
937	  % \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E, Leray's acyclicity lemma) and
938	  % Proposition \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
939	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5692--5705 and
940	  % L5785--5811).
941	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
942	  % between abelian categories. Let $A^\bullet$ be a bounded below complex
943	  % of right $F$-acyclic objects such that $RF$ is defined at
944	  % $A^\bullet$. The canonical map
945	  % $$
946	  % F(A^\bullet) \longrightarrow RF(A^\bullet)
947	  % $$
948	  % is an isomorphism in $D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$, i.e., $A^\bullet$ computes
949	  % $RF$."
950	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor of
951	  % abelian categories. ... If every object of $\mathcal{A}$ injects into an object
952	  % acyclic for $RF$, then $RF$ is defined on all of $K^{+}(\mathcal{A})$
953	  % and we obtain an exact functor
954	  % $$
955	  % RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^{+}(\mathcal{B})
956	  % $$
957	  % ... Moreover, any bounded below complex
958	  % $A^\bullet$ whose terms are acyclic for $RF$ computes $RF$."
959	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015E (Leray's acyclicity
960	  lemma) and Tag 05TA (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
961	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
962	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
963	  Let
964	  \[
965	    0 \longrightarrow A
966	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
967	      \longrightarrow \cdots
968	  \]
969	  be a resolution of \(A\) (an exact sequence) in which every object \(J^n\) is
970	  right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}. Then for
971	  every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\) at \(A\) is
972	  computed by the resolution: there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
973	  \[
974	    (R^n G)(A) \;\cong\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
975	  \]
976	  where \(G(J^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex
977	  \(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to G(J^2) \to \cdots\) obtained by applying \(G\) degreewise
978	  to the augmentation-dropped resolution and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th cohomology
979	  object.
980	\end{theorem}
981	
982	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
983	% $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
984	% $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
985	% for $n \geq n_0$. These sequences induce distinguished triangles
986	% $(J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1})$ in $D^+(\mathcal{A})$ by
987	% Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}.
988	% ... By Proposition \ref{proposition-derived-functor} we have a
989	% distinguished triangle $(RF(J^n), RF(I^{n + 1}), RF(J^{n + 1}))$.
990	% Since $J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1}$ are in $\mathcal{I}$
991	% the long exact cohomology sequence
992	% ... associated to this distinguished triangle collapses
993	% to an exact sequence
994	% $$
995	% 0 \to F(J^n) \to F(I^{n + 1}) \to F(J^{n + 1}) \to 0
996	% $$
997	% This in turn proves that $F(I^\bullet)$ is exact."
998	\begin{proof}
999	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution,
1000	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker,
1001	    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
1002	    \leanok
1003	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, now assembled from the cosyzygy
1004	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
1005	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
1006	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by
1007	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}.
1008	
1009	  \emph{Degree zero.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution},
1010	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
1011	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
1012	  This is the case \(n = 0\).
1013	
1014	  \emph{Positive degrees.} Fix \(n \geq 1\). The sequences
1015	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1016	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1017	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)
1018	  (with \(Z^0 = A\)). Composing them down the staircase
1019	  \[
1020	    (R^n G)(A)
1021	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1023	      \;\cong\; \cdots
1024	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})
1025	  \]
1026	  reduces \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor. (For \(n = 1\) the
1027	  staircase is empty and \((R^1 G)(A) = (R^1 G)(Z^0)\).) Now apply the lowest-degree
1028	  cokernel description (Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}) to \((S_{n-1})\),
1029	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), whose middle term \(J^{n-1}\) is
1030	  acyclic:
1031	  \[
1032	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
1033	  \]
1034	  Finally Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution} identifies this cokernel
1035	  with the cohomology of the applied complex,
1036	  \(\operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\). Composing
1037	  the staircase, the cokernel description, and this identification gives
1038	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for every \(n \geq 1\); together with the
1039	  degree-zero case this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
1040	
1041	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the dimension-shift
1042	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} and its lowest-degree companion
1043	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
1044	  horseshoe lift and the complex-level homology long exact sequence on which those
1045	  lemmas rest --- on the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\). The injective
1046	  objects supplied by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
1047	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
1048	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
1049	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
1050	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
1051	\end{proof}
1052

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, definition of $R^i f_*$ as the
5	% $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex
6	% and references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter records the derived-functor higher direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\)
11	for \(i \geq 1\). It provides the single object that the {\v C}ech computation of
12	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} compares against: the comparison
13	theorem \(\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}\) identifies the cohomology of the
14	relative {\v C}ech complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) wherever the derived functor
15	is defined.
16	
17	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category).
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
19	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	
21	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
22	
23	\begin{definition}\leanok
24	[Higher direct image]
25	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
26	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
27	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
28	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
29	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
30	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
31	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
32	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
33	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
34	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
35	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
36	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
37	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
38	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
39	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
40	  % the sheaf
41	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
42	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
43	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
44	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
45	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
46	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
47	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
48	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
49	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
50	  sheafification of the presheaf
51	  \[
52	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
53	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
54	  \]
55	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
56	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
57	\end{definition}
58

22	\section{Setup and motivation}
23	
24	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
25	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
26	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
27	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
28	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
29	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
30	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
31	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
32	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
33	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
34	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
35	
36	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
37	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
38	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
39	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
40	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
41	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
42	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
43	\[
44	  \begin{array}{ccc}
45	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
46	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
47	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
48	  \end{array}
49	\]
50	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
51	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
52	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
53	
54	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
55	
56	\begin{definition}
57	
58	\leanok
59	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
60	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
62	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
63	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
64	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
65	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
66	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
67	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
68	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
69	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
70	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
71	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
72	  % the unit ideal, see
73	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
74	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
75	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
76	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
77	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
78	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
79	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
80	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
81	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
82	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
83	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
84	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
85	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
86	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
87	  \[
88	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
89	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
90	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
91	  \]
92	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
93	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
94	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
95	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
96	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
97	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
98	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
99	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
100	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
101	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
102	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
103	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
104	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
105	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
106	\end{definition}
107	
108	\begin{definition}
109	
110	\leanok
111	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
112	  \label{def:cech_complex}
113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
114	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
115	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
116	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
117	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
118	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
119	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
120	  % is identified with the complex
121	  % $$
122	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to

124	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to

133	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
134	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
135	  % \ldots
136	  % \end{equation}

138	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
139	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
140	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
141	  vanishing).}
142	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
143	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
144	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
145	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
146	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
147	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
148	  in degree \(p\) it is
149	  \[
150	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
151	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
152	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
153	  \]
154	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
155	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
156	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
157	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
158	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
159	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
160	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
161	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
162	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
163	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
164	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
165	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
166	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
167	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
168	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
169	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
170	\end{definition}

172	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
173	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
174	
175	It is convenient to factor the construction of
176	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
177	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
178	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
179	
180	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
181	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
182	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
183	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
184	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
185	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
186	\[
187	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
188	    \;=\;
189	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
190	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
191	\]
192	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
193	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
194	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
195	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
196	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
197	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
198	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
199	
200	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
Let me continue reading the file in chunks.

200	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
201	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
202	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
203	\[
204	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
205	  \qquad
206	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
207	\]
208	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
209	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
210	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
211	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
212	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
213	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
214	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
215	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
216	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
217	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
218	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
219	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
220	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
221	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
222	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
223	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
224	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
225	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
226	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
227	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
228	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
229	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
230	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
231	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
232	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
233	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
234	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
235	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
236	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
237	
238	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
239	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
240	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
241	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
242	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
243	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
244	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
245	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
246	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
247	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
248	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
249	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
250	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
251	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
252	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
253	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
254	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
255	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
256	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
257	
258	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
259	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
260	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
261	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
262	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
263	
264	\begin{definition}\leanok
265	[Arrow of an affine cover]
266	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
268	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
269	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
270	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
271	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
272	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
273	  apply.
274	\end{definition}
275	
276	\begin{definition}[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
277	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
278	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOver, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOverAug}
279	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
280	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
281	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
282	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
283	  \[
284	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
285	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
286	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
287	  \]
288	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
289	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
290	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
291	\end{definition}
292	
293	\begin{definition}\leanok
294	[Push--pull object map]
295	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
296	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
297	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
298	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
299	  \[
300	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
301	  \]
302	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
303	\end{definition}
304	
305	\begin{definition}[Push--pull comparison map]
306	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
308	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
309	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
310	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
311	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
312	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
313	  \[
314	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
315	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
316	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
317	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
318	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
319	      \;=\;
320	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
321	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
322	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
323	  \]
324	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
325	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
326	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
327	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
328	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
329	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
330	\end{definition}

332	\begin{lemma}
333	
334	\leanok
335	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
336	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
337	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
338	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
339	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
340	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
341	\end{lemma}
342	
343	\begin{proof}
344	
345	\leanok
346	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
347	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
348	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
349	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
350	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
351	\end{proof}
352	
353	\begin{lemma}
354	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
355	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
356	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_pentagon}
357	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
358	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
359	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
360	  \[
361	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
362	  \]
363	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
364	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
365	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
366	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
367	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
368	\end{lemma}
369	
370	\begin{proof}
371	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
372	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
373	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
374	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
375	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
376	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
377	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
378	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
379	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
380	\end{proof}

382	\begin{lemma}
383	
384	\leanok
385	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
386	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
387	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
388	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
389	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
390	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
391	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
392	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
393	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
394	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
395	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
396	  \[
397	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
398	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
399	    \;=\;
400	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
401	  \]
402	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
403	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
404	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
405	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
406	\end{lemma}
407	
408	\begin{proof}
409	
410	\leanok
411	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
412	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
413	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
414	\end{proof}

416	\begin{lemma}
417	
418	\leanok
419	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
420	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
421	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
422	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
423	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the

427	  collapses to the transport-light form

430	      \;\circ\; e_2
431	    \;=\;
432	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
433	      \;\circ\; e_3,
434	  \]
435	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by

438	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
439	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
440	  composition (pentagon) law.
441	\end{lemma}
442	
443	\begin{proof}
444	
445	\leanok
446	  Substitute the over-triangle equality \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\) to replace
447	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
448	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.

504	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
505	
506	\begin{definition}
507	[Standard affine cover from a spanning family]
508	  \label{def:standard_affine_cover}
509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}
510	  \mathlibok
511	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cover.Open}).}
512	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring (so \(R = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R,
513	  \mathcal{O})\)) and let \(s : \iota \to R\) be a family of elements that generate
514	  the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\). The
515	  \emph{standard affine open cover} of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) associated with
516	  \(s\) is the affine open cover whose index set is \(\iota\), whose \(i\)-th member
517	  is the basic open \(D(s_i)\), and whose \(i\)-th structure morphism is the map of
518	  affine schemes induced by the localisation homomorphism \(R \to R_{s_i}\); that
519	  is, in Mathlib notation the defining equation
520	  \(\bigl(\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\bigr)_f\, i =
521	  \operatorname{Spec.map}\bigl(\text{algebraMap}\; R\; (\operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i))\bigr)\),
522	  so that the sections over the \(i\)-th piece are the \emph{away} localisation
523	  \(R_{s_i} = \operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i)\) and the geometric piece is
524	  \(D(s_i)\). This is exactly the standard open covering
525	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_i D(s_i)\) by basic opens, packaged as a single
526	  cover object. It is the spanning-family bundle \((s, hs)\) that the affine
527	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.
528	\end{definition}

530	\subsection{The categorical--module bridge (L1)}
531	\label{sec:cech_l1_bridge}
532	
533	The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
534	\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
535	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
536	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
537	concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
538	\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
539	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
540	restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
541	reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
542	underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
543	Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
544	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
545	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
546	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
547	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.
548	
549	\begin{definition}
550	
551	[Quasi-coherent sections over a basic open are an away localisation]
552	  \label{def:qcoh_sections_localized}
553	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcohSectionsAwayLocalized,
554	    AlgebraicGeometry.basicOpen_sprod,
555	    AlgebraicGeometry.iInf_fin_succ,
556	    AlgebraicGeometry.qcohRestriction_eq_comparison,
557	    AlgebraicGeometry.tP,
558	    AlgebraicGeometry.tU,
559	    AlgebraicGeometry.phiL,
560	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi,
561	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi_eq_phiL,
562	    AlgebraicGeometry.restr_bridge}
563	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:standard_affine_cover}
564	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
565	  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L701--707).
566	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
567	  % as an $R_f$-module.
568	  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
569	  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
570	  % are the maps
571	  % $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
572	  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
573	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
574	  items (4)--(5).}
575	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
576	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module, and write
577	  \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\) for its module of global
578	  sections. For every \(g \in R\), the restriction-of-sections map
579	  \(M = \mathcal{F}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g))\) exhibits the
580	  section group \(\mathcal{F}(D(g))\) as the away localisation \(M_g\) of \(M\); that
581	  is, this map is an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the multiplicative
582	  submonoid \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g\) of powers of \(g\). Moreover, for
583	  \(D(g_2) \subseteq D(g_1)\) the restriction map
584	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(g_1)) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g_2))\) is, under these identifications, the
585	  canonical localisation map \(M_{g_1} \to M_{g_2}\) (the unique \(R\)-linear map
586	  compatible with the localisation units). Item~(4) of Tag~01HV gives the first
587	  assertion for \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) (\(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\)
588	  as an \(R_g\)-module) and item~(5) gives the second (the restriction along
589	  \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\)).
590	
591	  The module-level input on the side of Mathlib is the development
592	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}: the comparison map
593	  \((\operatorname{tilde.toOpen} M\, (D(g))).\mathrm{hom}\) carries the instance
594	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g)\), and
595	  \(\operatorname{isUnit\_algebraMap\_end\_basicOpen}\) records that \(g\) acts
596	  invertibly on the sections over \(D(g)\); the restriction compatibility is
597	  \(\operatorname{tilde.toOpen\_res}\). These supply the statement \emph{verbatim} for
598	  the standard sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\). For an \emph{arbitrary} quasi-coherent
599	  \(\mathcal{F}\) the remaining step is the canonical comparison
600	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})}\), i.e.\
601	  that the counit \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism. This
602	  is \emph{not} yet available in Mathlib for a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\):
603	  Mathlib provides it only from a \emph{global} presentation
604	  (\(\operatorname{isIso\_fromTildeΓ\_of\_presentation}\)), whereas quasi-coherence
605	  supplies only \emph{local} presentation data on a cover. Globalising the local data
606	  on the affine \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) --- the affine equivalence
607	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \simeq \operatorname{Mod}(R)\), Stacks
608	  Tag~01I8 --- is the one genuine gap this definition records as a build target; it is
609	  the canonical missing affine equivalence and is self-contained.
610	
611	  Two combinatorial--geometric ingredients package the identification into the form
612	  consumed by Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}. First, the multi-index
613	  intersection identity
614	  \(\bigl(\bigcap_k D(s_{\sigma(k)})\bigr) = D\bigl(\prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\bigr)\) in
615	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R).\mathrm{Opens}\) (\(\operatorname{basicOpen\_sprod}\), proved
616	  by induction on the index length using the infimum-splitting helper
617	  \(\operatorname{iInf\_fin\_succ}\) and \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen\_mul}\)):
618	  it rewrites the \((p+1)\)-fold intersection over a multi-index \(\sigma\) as the single
619	  basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\), so the
620	  section group there is the away localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). Second, the restriction
621	  compatibility of item~(5) is made explicit: for a basic-open inclusion
622	  \(D(b) \subseteq D(a)\) the presheaf restriction map of \(\widetilde{M}\) between the
623	  two section groups \emph{is} the away-localisation comparison
624	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison}\) of the two localisation units
625	  (\(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\), by the uniqueness
626	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison\_unique}\) of a map of localised modules);
627	  this is the single differential brick that the {\v C}ech coface match of
628	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} feeds on.
629	
630	  The identification is packaged by a few helpers: the two localised-module endpoints
631	  \(\operatorname{tP}, \operatorname{tU}\) (the section groups
632	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) named as away localisations at the two indices
633	  involved in a face), the per-\(\sigma\) comparison
634	  \(\varphi_\sigma = \operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.iso}\) realised both as the bare
635	  localisation comparison \(\operatorname{phiL}\) and as its sheaf-level avatar
636	  \(\operatorname{phi}\) (their agreement is \(\operatorname{phi\_eq\_phiL}\)), and the
637	  accessor-bridge \(\operatorname{restr\_bridge}\) that exhibits the presheaf
638	  restriction map through these comparisons.
639	\end{definition}
640	
641	\begin{lemma}
642	[Section {\v C}ech homology vanishes iff the localised complex is exact]
643	  \label{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}
644	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_homology_exact,
645	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts,
646	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.isUnit_powers,
647	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.mul,
648	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.of_dvd,
649	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison,
650	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_apply,
651	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp,
652	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp_apply,
653	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_comp_structure,
654	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_self,
655	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_unique,
656	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoeff,
657	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoeff_transport_eq_comparison,
658	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechCoface,
659	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechLocalized_exact,
660	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cechPrepend,
661	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hcomm,
662	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hsh,
663	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.cech_hu,
664	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod,
665	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod_cons,
666	    AlgebraicGeometry.CechLocalized.sprod_succAbove_dvd}
667	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized, lem:section_cech_module_exact,
668	    lem:section_cech_product_equiv, lem:section_cech_objd_apply,
669	    lem:section_cech_coface_match, lem:section_cech_ab_exact}
670	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
671	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (the {\v C}ech complex is
672	  % the complex of localisations) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
673	  % L58--67).
674	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
675	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
676	  % is identified with the complex
677	  % $$
678	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
679	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
680	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
681	  % \ldots
682	  % $$"
683	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
684	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (identification with the
685	  complex of localisations).}
686	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
687	  R}\)-module and \(\mathcal{U}\) the standard cover associated with a spanning family
688	  \(s : \iota \to R\). Under the section identification of
689	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}, the section {\v C}ech complex
690	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
691	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} is isomorphic, as a cochain complex, to
692	  the concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules
693	  \[
694	    D^\bullet : \quad
695	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
696	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0,1\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
697	    \cdots,
698	    \qquad
699	    s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
700	  \]
701	  whose differential is the alternating sum of the canonical localisation maps. The
702	  intended top-level conclusion is the directed homology vanishing
703	  \[
704	    1 \leq p
705	    \;\Longrightarrow\;
706	    \operatorname{IsZero}\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
707	      \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p\bigr),
708	  \]
709	  which is the form the affine vanishing
710	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} (\(\operatorname{sectionCech\_affine\_vanishing}\))
711	  consumes; it is read off from positive-degree exactness of the underlying group
712	  homomorphisms,
713	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to D^{p+1}\bigr)\), which is
714	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.
715	
716	  The reduction from the abstract homology to this group-level exactness is \emph{not}
717	  a \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\) statement: the degree-\(p\) object of
718	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) is the \emph{categorical
719	  product}
720	  \(\prod^{c}_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}
721	  \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) in the category \(\mathrm{Ab}\) of abelian groups (the
722	  alternating-coface-map complex of the \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-valued cosimplicial object
723	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\)), not an \(R\)-module object, so the
724	  module-category criterion \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.moduleCat\_exact\_iff}\) does
725	  not apply. The correct chain runs through three steps, isolated as the sub-lemmas
726	  below: \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology is \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) at
727	  \(p\) (\(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\)); \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\)
728	  of a short complex in \(\mathrm{Ab}\) is the underlying-group exactness
729	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\) via the abelian
730	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
731	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}); and the categorical product is moved to the
732	  per-\(\sigma\) localised modules by the element-level product equivalence
733	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
734	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\)
735	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}), under which the {\v C}ech coface
736	  differential matches the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
737	  \(D^\bullet\) (the abstract cosimplicial unfold
738	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} followed by the tilde-bridge identification
739	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). Transporting the
740	  already-proved exactness \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of
741	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} across the product equivalence then
742	  discharges the group-level exactness. The active target is the tilde case
743	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), for which the per-coordinate comparisons are
744	  immediate from \(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\); a general
745	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) is reduced to this case via the affine equivalence
746	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma\mathcal{F}}\) (Stacks Tag~01I8), the
747	  separately-deferred globalisation gap recorded in
748	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}.
749	\end{lemma}
750	
751	\begin{proof}
752	  \uses{def:qcoh_sections_localized, def:section_cech_complex}
753	  The term identification is the degreewise statement of
754	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the degree-\(p\) section group
755	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is \(\prod_\sigma M_{s_\sigma}\), since
756	  each \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap
757	  D(s_{\sigma(p)})\) is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
758	  \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\) and the sections there are the away
759	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). The differential identification is item~(5) of
760	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: each face restriction
761	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})) \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is the
762	  canonical localisation map \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\), so the
763	  alternating sum of restrictions is the alternating sum of localisation maps, the
764	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) (the coface match of
765	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). The homology vanishing then follows in
766	  three steps. First, \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) turns
767	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology at \(p\) into \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of
768	  the short complex at \(p\). Second, because the degree-\(p\) objects are
769	  \emph{categorical products in \(\mathrm{Ab}\)} rather than \(R\)-modules, the abelian
770	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
771	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}) --- not the module-category criterion ---
772	  reduces \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) to the underlying-group exactness
773	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\). Third, the
774	  element-level product equivalence
775	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
776	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
777	  identifies each product group with the per-\(\sigma\) localised module \(D^p\), so the
778	  group-level exactness is exactly \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to
779	  D^{p+1})\), supplied by \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\)
780	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}).
781	
782	  The identification of the abstract section complex with the concrete localised
783	  complex \(D^\bullet\) --- together with the construction of the prepend transport
784	  \(c\) and the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\) discharging the unit, shift, and
785	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\)/\(\mathtt{hsh}\)/\(\mathtt{hcomm}\)
786	  of the dependent combinatorial port --- is the bulk of the bridge. The non-circular
787	  route is reflection along the localisation equivalence on its essential image (the
788	  global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is the inverse equivalence there, so it reflects
789	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of homology); it is \emph{not} the assertion that
790	  ``\(\Gamma\) preserves homology'', which would presuppose the very affine exactness
791	  being proved.
792	\end{proof}
793	
794	\begin{lemma}
795	
796	[Element-level product equivalence for the section {\v C}ech terms]
797	  \label{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
798	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechProductEquiv,
799	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechProductEquiv_apply,
800	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionProdAddEquiv}
801	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
802	  Let \(\{F_\sigma\}_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}\) be the finite family of
803	  abelian groups \(F_\sigma = \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) appearing in degree \(p\) of
804	  the section {\v C}ech complex of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}. There is
805	  an equivalence of underlying types
806	  \[
807	    \operatorname{ToType}\Bigl(\prod^{c}_{\sigma} F_\sigma\Bigr)
808	    \;\simeq\;
809	    \prod_{\sigma} \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma),
810	  \]
811	  between the elements of the categorical product \(\prod^{c}_{\sigma} F_\sigma\) in
812	  \(\mathrm{Ab}\) and the dependent functions \(\sigma \mapsto\) (element of
813	  \(F_\sigma\)). It is supplied by the Mathlib concrete-limit comparison
814	  \(\operatorname{CategoryTheory.Limits.Concrete.productEquiv}\), which holds because the
815	  forgetful functor \(\operatorname{forget}\,\mathrm{Ab}\) preserves the discrete
816	  product (\(\operatorname{PreservesLimit}(\operatorname{Discrete.functor} F)\)). Under
817	  this equivalence each coordinate
818	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma)))\) is identified with the away
819	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma} = \operatorname{dCoeff}\) of
820	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} by the
821	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) uniqueness of
822	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} (both
823	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) and \(M_{s_\sigma}\) localise \(M\) at \(s_\sigma\), so
824	  the comparison is the canonical isomorphism). This is the move between the
825	  categorical product of \(\mathrm{Ab}\) and the per-\(\sigma\) localised modules.
826	  Its packaging as an additive isomorphism --- the degreewise vertical map of the
827	  ladder transport in Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact} --- is recorded as
828	  \(\operatorname{sectionProdAddEquiv}\), the \(\operatorname{AddEquiv}\) underlying
829	  the coordinatewise comparison.
830	  The coordinate projection of the equivalence is recorded separately as the
831	  definitional restatement
832	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv\_apply}\): the \(\sigma\)-component of the
833	  image of \(y\) under the equivalence is the underlying group map of the categorical
834	  projection \(\pi_\sigma\) applied to \(y\).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
839	  The type-level equivalence is \(\operatorname{Concrete.productEquiv}\) applied to the
840	  family \(\sigma \mapsto F_\sigma\); its hypothesis is that
841	  \(\operatorname{forget}\,\mathrm{Ab}\) preserves the discrete-diagram limit, which is
842	  the standard instance for \(\mathrm{Ab}\). The per-coordinate identification
843	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))) \cong M_{s_\sigma}\) is the
844	  uniqueness clause of \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\): by
845	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} the restriction map
846	  \(M \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) and the localisation unit
847	  \(M \to M_{s_\sigma}\) are both localisations of \(M\) at
848	  \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers}(s_\sigma)\)
849	  (\(\operatorname{qcohSectionsAwayLocalized}\)), hence canonically isomorphic over
850	  \(M\). Assembling the coordinatewise isomorphisms after the product equivalence gives
851	  the stated identification.
852	\end{proof}
853	
854	\begin{lemma}
855	[Abstract cosimplicial unfold of the section {\v C}ech differential]
856	  \label{lem:section_cech_objd_apply}
857	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_objD_apply,
858	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechFaceRestr}
859	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
860	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
861	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open cover. For a multi-index
862	  \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(q+2) \to I\) and a face index
863	  \(i \in \mathrm{Fin}(q+2)\), write
864	  \[
865	    \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i) :
866	      \mathcal{F}\Bigl(\,\bigcap_{l} U_{(\sigma \circ d_i)(l)}\Bigr)
867	      \;\longrightarrow\;
868	      \mathcal{F}\Bigl(\,\bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)}\Bigr)
869	  \]
870	  for the \(i\)-th presheaf face restriction --- the value of \(\mathcal{F}\) on the
871	  inclusion of opens
872	  \(\bigcap_{k} U_{\sigma(k)} \subseteq \bigcap_{l} U_{(\sigma \circ d_i)(l)}\)
873	  obtained from the order-preserving coface \(d_i\) of the simplex category. Then,
874	  read through the product equivalence
875	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}\) of
876	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}, the underlying group action of the
877	  degree-\(q\) differential
878	  \(\operatorname{objD} q\) of the alternating-coface-map complex
879	  \(\operatorname{AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex}
880	  (\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\,\mathcal{U}\,\mathcal{F})\) is the alternating
881	  sum of the face restrictions applied to the deleted-face coordinates:
882	  \[
883	    \bigl(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_{q+1}\,(\operatorname{objD} q\, t)\bigr)_\sigma
884	      \;=\;
885	      \sum_{i = 0}^{q+1} (-1)^i \,
886	        \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)
887	          \Bigl(\bigl(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}_{q}\, t\bigr)_{\sigma \circ d_i}\Bigr).
888	  \]
889	  This is \emph{purely cosimplicial}: it records the shape of the section {\v C}ech
890	  differential as an alternating sum of presheaf restrictions, with \emph{no}
891	  sheaf-theoretic or localisation identification of those restrictions. It is the
892	  abstract shape that the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
893	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} matches once the tilde-bridge
894	  identification of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match} is supplied.
895	\end{lemma}

897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}

4824	\begin{lemma}[Scalar tower on a bundled restriction-of-scalars module]
4825	  \label{lem:isScalarTower_restrictScalars_obj}
4826	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isScalarTower_restrictScalars_obj}
4827	  Let \(R \to S\) be a ring map and let \(M\) be an \(S\)-module. Then the \(R\)-module obtained from
4828	  \(M\) by restriction of scalars along \(R \to S\), written
4829	  \((\mathrm{restrictScalars}_{R \to S})\,M\), carries a scalar-tower structure: the restricted
4830	  \(R\)-action factors through the \(S\)-action via the algebra map \(R \to S\).
4831	\end{lemma}
4832	\begin{proof}
4833	  By definition of restriction of scalars, the \(R\)-action on \(M\) is given by
4834	  \(r \cdot m = \phi(r) \cdot m\) where \(\phi : R \to S\) is the algebra map. This is precisely the
4835	  scalar-tower identity: the \(R\)-action factors through the \(S\)-action via \(\phi\). Hence \(R\),
4836	  \(S\), and \(\phi\) form a scalar tower on \((\mathrm{restrictScalars}_{R \to S})\,M\).
4837	\end{proof}

4839	\begin{lemma}[Reconciliation equivalence for the tile section comparison]
4840	  \label{lem:tileReconcileEquiv}
4841	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tileReconcileEquiv, AlgebraicGeometry.tileReconcileEquiv_apply,
4842	    AlgebraicGeometry.tileReconcileEquiv_symm_apply}
4843	  \uses{lem:tile_scalar_compat_genV, lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq}
4844	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}\)-module, let \(g \in R\), write \(R_g\)
4845	  for the localisation of \(R\) away from \(g\), and let \(V \subseteq \Spec R_g\) be an open. Let
4846	  \(\iota : \Spec R_g \xrightarrow{\sim} D(g) \hookrightarrow \Spec R\) be the localisation open
4847	  immersion, so that \(\iota(V)\) is the iterated image of \(V\) under the affine identification
4848	  \(\Spec R_g \cong D(g)\) followed by the open inclusion \(D(g) \hookrightarrow \Spec R\). Then there is
4849	  an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
4850	  \[
4851	    (\mathrm{restrictScalars}_{R \to R_g})\, \Gamma_{R_g}\bigl(V, \mathcal{F}_{(g)}\bigr)
4852	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
4853	      \Gamma_R\bigl(\iota(V),\ \mathcal{F}\bigr),
4854	  \]
4855	  which is the identity on underlying elements. The forward and inverse maps are each the identity on
4856	  elements, so the only content is the reconciliation of the two module structures carried by the
4857	  common section type.
4858	\end{lemma}
4859	\begin{proof}
4860	  \uses{lem:tile_scalar_compat_genV, lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq}
4861	  The two section modules share one underlying carrier: the tile sections over \(V\) coincide as
4862	  sets with the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the image open \(\iota(V)\), by construction of the
4863	  restriction functor. The underlying map is therefore the identity, and it is additive. Its \(R\)-linearity is
4864	  exactly the general-open scalar-tower compatibility of Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_scalar_compat_genV}: the
4865	  native \(R\)-action on the \(\mathcal{F}\)-side (described by
4866	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq}) coincides with the \(R_g\)-action read through the
4867	  localisation map \(R \to R_g\), i.e.\ the restriction-of-scalars action carried by the tile side. The
4868	  identity map is thus \(R\)-linear with \(R\)-linear inverse, hence an \(R\)-linear isomorphism.
4869	\end{proof}

4900	\begin{proof}
4901	  \uses{lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen, lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation,
4902	    lem:restrict_obj_mathlib, lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_restrictScalars_of_algebraMap,
4903	    lem:tile_image_opens_identities, lem:tile_scalar_compat_genV, lem:tileReconcileEquiv,

4906	  % NOTE (review iter-046): proof LANDED axiom-clean (see statement-block NOTE); the five-step descent
4907	  % below is the formalized route. \leanok is the genuine sync verdict.
4908	  % NOTE: the naive route using the carrier equality of Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib}
4909	  % is UNSOUND: that equality holds only for the local-ring section functor,
4910	  % whereas the localisation here uses the global-ring section functor,
4911	  % which does NOT commute with restriction definitionally. Hence the five-step descent below.
4912	  Let \(\iota : \Spec R_g \xrightarrow{\sim} D(g) \hookrightarrow X\) be the open immersion of
4913	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}, with \(\bar f\) the image of \(f\) under the
4914	  localisation map \(R \to R_g\). The proof is a base-ring descent in five steps; the section
4915	  comparison it rests on is \emph{not} the definitional equality of
4916	  Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib} (see the proof of
4917	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_comparison}).
4918	
4919	  \emph{Step 1 (global presentation of the tile).} Since \(D(g)\) lies in a presentation member of the
4920	  cover, step B4 (Lemma~\ref{lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen}) presents the tile
4921	  \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) globally over \(\Spec R_g\).
4922	
4923	  \emph{Step 2 (localisation over \(R_g\)).} Apply
4924	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation} to the globally presented
4925	  \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) and \(\bar f\): the section-restriction map
4926	  \(\Gamma_{R_g}(\Spec R_g, \mathcal{F}_{(g)}) \to \Gamma_{R_g}(D(\bar f), \mathcal{F}_{(g)})\)
4927	  exhibits its target as a localisation at the powers of \(\bar f\), \emph{as \(R_g\)-modules}. Note this
4928	  is over \(R_g\), not yet over \(R\).
4929	
4930	  \emph{Step 3 (opens identities).} By Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_image_opens_identities} the two image opens of
4931	  \(\iota\) are \(\iota(\Spec R_g) = D(g)\) and \(\iota(D(\bar f)) = D(gf) = D(g) \cap D(f)\).
4932	
4933	  \emph{Step 4 (the section comparison, as a restriction of scalars).} The localisation of Step 2 lives
4934	  over \(R_g\); it must be related to the section-restriction map of \(\mathcal{F}\) over \(R\). The two
4935	  maps share the same underlying map of section sets: this is the carrier identification of
4936	  Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib}, now read as an equality of \(R\)-linear maps (with the
4937	  \(\mathcal{F}\)-side open kept in its iterated-image form \(W = \iota(V)\)). The one genuine difference
4938	  is the base ring: the tile sections carry an \(R_g\)-module structure, while the
4939	  \(\mathcal{F}\)-sections carry an \(R\)-module structure. The descent that reconciles them is precisely
4940	  the \emph{restriction of scalars} along the localisation map \(R \to R_g\): each tile section module,
4941	  viewed through restriction of scalars, becomes an \(R\)-module, and the Step-2 map so viewed is the
4942	  section-restriction map \(\Gamma_R(D(g), \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma_R(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\).
4943	
4944	  For this restriction of scalars to be meaningful, the native \(R\)-action on each section must agree
4945	  with its \(R_g\)-action read through \(R \to R_g\); this is the scalar-tower compatibility. At the
4946	  source open \(V = \top\) (image \(D(g)\)) it is exactly Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_scalar_compat}, whose two
4947	  scalar prescriptions agree by the scalar-reduction identities
4948	  (Lemmas~\ref{lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq} and \ref{lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq}). At the
4949	  target open \(V = D(\bar f)\) (image \(D(gf)\)) it is the general-open companion
4950	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_scalar_compat_genV}, the \(V = D(\bar f)\) instance of the same ring-naturality
4951	  content. Both opens of the Step-2 map are thus covered. In the descent these two scalar
4952	  reconciliations are packaged uniformly as the \(R\)-linear reconciliation isomorphism of
4953	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tileReconcileEquiv} (instantiated at the source and target opens), whose
4954	  \(R\)-linearity is supplied by the general-open compatibility Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_scalar_compat_genV};
4955	  the descent transports the Step-2 localisation through these isomorphisms.
4956	
4957	  Reading Step 2's localisation-at-\(\bar f\) property through restriction of scalars along
4958	  \(R \to R_g\), and matching source and target opens by Step 3, yields: the map
4959	  \(\Gamma_R(D(g), \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma_R(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\) exhibits its target as a localisation
4960	  at the powers of \(\bar f\), \emph{still as \(R_g\)-modules}.
4961	
4962	  \emph{Step 5 (base-ring descent to \(R\)).} Finally descend the base ring from \(R_g\) to \(R\) by
4963	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_restrictScalars_of_algebraMap}, applied with \(A = R_g\) and the
4964	  scalar tower \(R \to R_g\) on the two section modules (supplied structurally on the bundled
4965	  restriction-of-scalars carriers by Lemma~\ref{lem:isScalarTower_restrictScalars_obj}): since \(\bar f\) is the image of \(f\) under
4966	  \(R \to R_g\), the powers of \(\bar f\) descend to the powers of \(f\), and the restriction of scalars
4967	  (from \(R_g\) to \(R\)) of the Step-4 map exhibits \(\Gamma_R(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\) as the localisation
4968	  of \(\Gamma_R(D(g), \mathcal{F})\) at the powers of \(f\) \emph{over \(R\)}. Equivalently there is a
4969	  canonical \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(\Gamma(D(g), \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\).
4970	
4971	  Crucially this localisation is carried out entirely on the tile \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\), where
4972	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is associated to a module (the tile is globally presented, hence of the form
4973	  \(\widetilde{M_g}\)), and never on
4974	  the global object \(\mathcal{F}\). This is exactly the structural feature that keeps the keystone
4975	  Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule} non-circular: the keystone-at-\(g\) statement is
4976	  \emph{not} invoked; only the presented-tile lemma is.
4977	\end{proof}
